name: David Koresh
description: David Koresh (born Vernon Howell) was the leader of a Branch Davidian religious sect near Waco, Texas, that ended in a deadly siege in 1993. Koresh joined the Branch Davidians in the 1980s and rose to prominence through charisma and strategic manipulation. He preached a complex theology centered on the Book of Revelation, claiming to be the Lamb of God prophesied to open the Seven Seals. He advocated for the stockpiling of weapons, interpreting biblical prophecies as necessitating an armed defense against perceived enemies. His followers believed in his divine authority and accepted his interpretations of scripture, even when these interpretations led to controversial practices, including polygamous marriages with underage girls, which Koresh justified through his interpretation of biblical texts. The ATF raid on the Mount Carmel Center in February 1993, prompted by suspicions of illegal weapons possession, initiated a 51-day standoff with federal authorities. The standoff culminated in a fire that destroyed the compound, resulting in the deaths of Koresh and approximately 75 of his followers, including children. Mainstream perspectives view Koresh as a manipulative cult leader who exploited religious beliefs for personal power and sexual gratification. Critics argue that his apocalyptic teachings and actions led to the tragic deaths of his followers. Government investigations following the siege have been controversial, with some accusing federal agencies of excessive force and mishandling the situation. However, most official reports attribute primary responsibility for the tragedy to Koresh's leadership and actions.
subtitle: Branch Davidian leader and Waco siege

claims.claim: Koresh was the Lamb of God prophesied in the Book of Revelation.
claims.evidence: Koresh's interpretation of the Seven Seals in Revelation, his charisma, and the devotion of his followers.
claims.criticism: Mainstream theology rejects Koresh's self-proclaimed messianic status. Biblical scholars point to inconsistencies and misinterpretations in his theological claims.

claims.claim: The Branch Davidians needed to arm themselves for an impending apocalyptic battle.
claims.evidence: Koresh's interpretation of biblical prophecies concerning the end times and the perceived threat from outside forces.
claims.criticism: Critics argue that Koresh used this belief to control his followers and justify the stockpiling of weapons, leading to the confrontation with law enforcement.

claims.claim: Koresh's sexual relationships with underage girls were justified by his interpretation of scripture.
claims.evidence: Koresh's interpretation of biblical figures marrying multiple women and his belief that he was fulfilling a divine mission to create a 'House of David'.
claims.criticism: These relationships are widely considered statutory rape and child abuse, violating both secular law and mainstream ethical standards. His interpretations are seen as self-serving rationalizations for his actions.

keyFigures.name: Janet Reno
keyFigures.role: U.S. Attorney General who authorized the final raid

timeline.event: Howell assumes leadership of the Branch Davidians and changes his name to David Koresh.
ATF raid on Mount Carmel Center.
FBI raid and fire at Mount Carmel Center; Koresh and many followers die.
